2. Other [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") (1.02%)
3. [No religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Poland "Irreligion in Poland") (6.87%)
4. Other religions (0.14%)
5. Undeclared (20.6%)

[![Exterior](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Praga%2C_Warsaw%2C_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_%2834%29.jpg/250px-Praga%2C_Warsaw%2C_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_%2834%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praga,_Warsaw,_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_(34).jpg)

[![Interior](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Praga%2C_Warsaw%2C_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_%283%29.jpg/250px-Praga%2C_Warsaw%2C_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praga,_Warsaw,_Poland_-_panoramio_-_Roman_Eugeniusz_(3).jpg)

[St. Florian's Roman Catholic Cathedral](/wiki/St._Florian%27s_Cathedral "St. Florian's Cathedral") in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"). A large majority of ethnic [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") are adherents of the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") branch of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").

**Religion in Poland** is [rapidly declining](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization"), although historically it had been one of the most [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") countries in the world.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)

According to a 2018 report by the *[Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center")*, the nation was the most rapidly secularizing of over a hundred countries measured, "as measured by the disparity between the religiosity of young people and their elders."[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The rate of decline has been described as "devastating"[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) the former social prestige and political influence that the [Catholic Church in Poland](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Poland "Catholic Church in Poland") once enjoyed.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Most Poles adhere to Roman Catholicism. 71.3% of the population identified themselves as such in the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Polish_census "2021 Polish census"), down from 87.6% in [2011](/wiki/2011_Polish_census "2011 Polish census").[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) According to church statistics, approximately 28% of Catholics attend mass weekly.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The church's reputation has declined significantly in response to [sexual abuse scandals](/wiki/Catholic_Church_sexual_abuse_cases "Catholic Church sexual abuse cases"), its support of [a near-total abortion ban in Poland](/wiki/Abortion_in_Poland "Abortion in Poland"), and close ties to the [Law and Justice](/wiki/Law_and_Justice_(Poland) "Law and Justice (Poland)") party, often considered its *de facto* political proxy in the country.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

The current extent of this numerical dominance results largely from [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") of Jews living in Poland carried out by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and the World War II casualties among Polish religious minorities.[[7]](#cite_note-posterum0-7)[[8]](#cite_note-5MillionForgotten0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-expatica-9)[[10]](#cite_note-szma-10) Its members regard it as a repository of Polish heritage and culture.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The rest of the population consists mainly of [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") ([Polish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church") – approximately 507,196 believers),[[12]](#cite_note-MRS-12) various [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") churches (the largest of which is the [Evangelical Church of the Augsburg Confession in Poland](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_the_Augsburg_Confession_in_Poland "Evangelical Church of the Augsburg Confession in Poland"), with 61,217 members)[[12]](#cite_note-MRS-12) and Jehovah's Witnesses (116,935).[[12]](#cite_note-MRS-12) There are about 55,000 [Greek Catholics](/wiki/Greek_Catholics "Greek Catholics") in Poland.[[12]](#cite_note-MRS-12) Other religions practiced in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), by less than 0.1% of the population, include [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Polish_census "2021 Polish census"), the most common religion was [Latin Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), whose followers comprised 71.3% of the population, followed by the [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") with 0.4%, [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") with 0.3%, and various Protestant denominations comprising 0.4% of the Polish population and 0.1% for [Greek Catholic](/wiki/Greek_Catholic "Greek Catholic") Churches. According to [Statistics Poland](/wiki/Statistics_Poland "Statistics Poland") in 2018, 93.5% of the population was affiliated with a religion; 3.1% did not belong to any religion. Roman Catholicism comprised 91.9% of the population, with [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") at 0.9% (rising from 0.4% in 2011, caused in part by recent immigration from [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")).[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

In 2015, 61.1% of the population gave religion high to very high importance whilst 13.8% regarded religion as of little or no importance. The percentage of believers is much higher in the eastern parts of Poland.[[15]](#cite_note-GUS2016-15)

## History

For centuries the ancient West Slavic and [Lechitic](/wiki/Lechites "Lechites") peoples inhabiting the lands of modern-day Poland have practiced various forms of [paganism](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") known as [Rodzimowierstwo](/wiki/Slavic_Native_Faith "Slavic Native Faith") (“*native faith*”).[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) From the beginning of its statehood, different religions coexisted in Poland. With the [baptism of Poland](/wiki/Baptism_of_Poland "Baptism of Poland") in 966, the old [pagan](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") religions were gradually eradicated over the next few centuries during the [Christianization of Poland](/wiki/Christianization_of_Poland "Christianization of Poland"). However, this did not put an end to pagan beliefs in the country. The persistence was demonstrated by a series of rebellions known as the [Pagan reaction](/wiki/Pagan_reaction_in_Poland "Pagan reaction in Poland") in the first half of the 11th century, which also showed elements of a peasant uprising against landowners and feudalism,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and led to a mutiny that destabilized the country.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) By the 13th century Catholicism had become the dominant religion throughout the country. Nevertheless, Christian Poles coexisted with a significant Jewish segment of the population.[[26]](#cite_note-Wandycz1980-26)[[27]](#cite_note-LukowskiZawadzki2006-27)

In the 15th century, the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars") and the pressure from the [papacy](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") led to religious tensions between Catholics and the emergent [Hussite](/wiki/Hussite "Hussite") and subsequent Protestant community, particularly after the [Edict of Wieluń](/wiki/Edict_of_Wielu%C5%84 "Edict of Wieluń") (1424).[[28]](#cite_note-MH-28) The [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") movement gained a significant following in Poland and, though Roman Catholicism retained a dominant position within the state, the liberal [Warsaw Confederation (1573)](/wiki/Warsaw_Confederation_(1573) "Warsaw Confederation (1573)") guaranteed wide [religious tolerance](/wiki/Religious_tolerance "Religious tolerance").[[28]](#cite_note-MH-28) But the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation")'s reactionary movement succeeded in reducing the scope for tolerance by the late 17th and early 18th century – as evidenced by events such as the [Tumult of Toruń](/wiki/Tumult_of_Thorn_(Toru%C5%84) "Tumult of Thorn (Toruń)") (1724).[[28]](#cite_note-MH-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Lerski1996-29)[[30]](#cite_note-UngerBasista2008-30)

When Poland [was divided](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") between its neighbors in the late eighteenth century, some Poles were subjected to religious discrimination in the newly expanded [German Prussia](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation") and [Russia](/wiki/Russification "Russification").[[31]](#cite_note-Cienciala-31)

Prior to the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), some 3,500,000 [Polish Jews](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews") (about 10% of the national population) lived in the [Polish Second Republic](/wiki/Polish_Second_Republic "Polish Second Republic"), largely in cities. Between the [Germano-Soviet invasions of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland_(1939) "Invasion of Poland (1939)") and the [end of World War II](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe "End of World War II in Europe"), over 90% of Jews in Poland perished.[[32]](#cite_note-Lukas-32) [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") (called the "[Shoah](/wiki/Shoah "Shoah")" in Hebrew) took the lives of more than three million mostly [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") Jews in Poland. Comparatively few managed to survive the [German occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%9345) "Occupation of Poland (1939–45)") or to escape eastward into the [territories of Poland annexed by the Soviet Union](/wiki/Territories_of_Poland_annexed_by_the_Soviet_Union "Territories of Poland annexed by the Soviet Union"), beyond the reach of the [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). As elsewhere in Europe during the [interwar](/wiki/Interwar "Interwar") period, there was both official and popular [anti-Semitism in Poland](/wiki/Anti-Semitism_in_Poland "Anti-Semitism in Poland"), at times encouraged by the Roman [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and by some political parties (particularly the right-wing *[endecja](/wiki/Endecja "Endecja")* and small ONR groups and factions), but not directly by the Polish government itself.[[33]](#cite_note-piotrowski-33)

According to a 2011 survey by [Ipsos MORI](/wiki/Ipsos_MORI "Ipsos MORI"), 85% of the [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") remain [Christians](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"); 8% are [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist"), or [agnostic](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic"); 2% adhere to unspecified other religions; and 5% did not answer the question.[[34]](#cite_note-IpsosMORI2011-34)

According to an opinion poll conducted in "a representative group of 1,000 people" by the [Centre for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/Centre_for_Public_Opinion_Research_(Poland) "Centre for Public Opinion Research (Poland)") (CBOS), published in 2015, 39% of Poles claim they are "believers following the Church's laws", while 52% answered that they are "believers in their own understanding and way", and 5% stated that they are [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism").[[35]](#cite_note-cbos2015-35)[[36]](#cite_note-oko.press-36)

## The Polish Constitution and religion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Religion_in_Poland.png/250px-Religion_in_Poland.png)](/wiki/File:Religion_in_Poland.png)

Percentage of persons who declared that they believe or very deeply believe, 2015.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[The Polish Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Constitution of the Republic of Poland") assures freedom of religion for all. State and religion are formally separated in Poland. The Constitution also grants national and ethnic minorities the rights to establish educational and cultural institutions and institutions designed to protect religious identity, as well as to participate in the resolution of matters connected with their cultural identities.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Religious organizations in the Republic of Poland can register their institution with the [Ministry of Interior and Administration](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_and_Administration_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Ministry of Interior and Administration of the Republic of Poland"), creating a record of churches and other religious organizations which operate under separate Polish laws. This registration is not necessary, but it does serve the laws guaranteeing freedom of religious practice.

Slavic [Rodzimowiercy](/wiki/Slavic_Neopaganism "Slavic Neopaganism") groups registered with the Polish authorities in 1995 are the [Native Polish Church](/wiki/Native_Polish_Church "Native Polish Church") (Rodzimy Kościół Polski), which represents a pagan tradition which goes back to pre-Christian faiths and continues [Władysław Kołodziej](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Ko%C5%82odziej "Władysław Kołodziej")'s 1921 Holy Circle of Worshipper of [Światowid](/wiki/Svetovid "Svetovid") (Święte Koło Czcicieli Światowida), and the Polish Slavic Church (Polski Kościół Słowiański).[[39]](#cite_note-39) This native Slavic religion is promoted also by the Native Faith Association (Zrzeszenie Rodzimej Wiary, ZRW), and the Association for Tradition founded in 2015.
